turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Number 64, Robert Swain, Petitioner, versus Alabama. Senator Motley.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice and may it please the Court. Petitioner in this case is a Negro male about 20 years of age. And he asked this Court to review the judgment of the Supreme Court of Alabama which affirmed his conviction and sentenced to death on a charge of rape. The petitioner was convicted of raping a 17-year-old White girl and was sentenced to death by a heavy jury sitting in Talladega County, Alabama. No Negro has ever served on a heavy jury in Talladega County because they are always struck from the trial jury venire by the states attorney.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Senator Motley does the -- in Alabama does the jury assess the penalty in a case of this time?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Within what range does -- do you know?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, no --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Again, they obviously can impose the death penalty.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, they did in this case assess the penalty of death, that's the penalty in Alabama.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: And it is -- in their discretion, apparently (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- the penalty?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: It's life or death sentence. Petitioner claimed below of course and asserts here that his conviction violates the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment and that the grand jury which indicted him in the petit jury which tried, convicted and sentenced him were the products of racial discrimination. Now, in a capital case in this county, the circuit judge orders the sheriff to summon at least 50 and not more than a 100 persons to serve on the petit jury. About 75 usually appear of the 100 that are summoned and of these numbers there will be some who claim exemptions and others will be excused for various reasons. And then of the number left, say there are 50 left, the striking then begins in this fashion, the states strikes one and the defendant strikes two.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Senator Motley, (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: So that if you have to (Inaudible)?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Is that in your case?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right sir, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, at the early stage, the -- counsel --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right, yes. The counsel can challenge of course, others will come in and claim exemption of those called to serve. And after all of those exemptions are claimed and all of the challenges proposed then you begin to strike from those that are left and it's usually as I say, 50, maybe 75 and you strike down until you get 12 and these well constitutes the jury.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And after that (Inaudible) in the course of having been eliminated (Inaudible).

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, that's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Going up to the last 12 --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right, the last 12 serve on the jury.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, it depends on the number left. You say, you don't have a set number --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: What --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right. It varies depending upon the number left of jurors after all exemptions and challenges of the Court, maybe 40 left or 50. The prosecutor strikes one, the defendant strikes two alternating until there are 12 left, until it -- will vary from case to case.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, they could talk (Inaudible) point where either one could strike (Inaudible)?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, there's a provision that if the defendant doesn't strike, the judge must strike for him in the Alabama law.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible) what I had in mind was, supposed both the prosecution and defense counsel, they have 12 in the box and while there are still 20 or 30 on the list, neither want to strike anymore, may that happen?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Oh, I guess you can accept the box, yes, which --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: -- of the 12, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: There -- did they have a special panel summoned for capital cases in venire?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, what they do is to summon additional jurors in addition to those who are summoned for the week. And this may not exceed a 100 in number and of course if they run out they always summon additional jurors to serve.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, the statute only provided that -- those compelled to have a special panel, is that still the law there?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, its -- yes, I think so. I think what happens is that this extra number that are called in addition to those that are there for the regular trial for the week or who have already been called is what they referred to as a special venire for a capital case because they usually do not have as many as a 100 there for the trial of a case for that week.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: What happens in (Inaudible)?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, then they would --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Before trial?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: You mean, if they have -- I don't --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Would you agree then, they've gone through this process and they seek to select the jurors, a problem comes out (Inaudible) the challenge, if the challenge (Inaudible)?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: You say there are no more jurors left to strike from? I don't think --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: 12 in that (Inaudible)?

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Its going to work that way, there's (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: The -- no, no.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: They begin with 30 or 40.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: And then you reduce it to twelve.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: On a one-to-two basis?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes. I didn't understand your question Your Honor. I thought I had explained this but I will say it again. The -- between 50 and a 100 are called to serve, come in, some will claim exempt from this, they are over 65 or under 21. Certain occupations are exempt. Then, others will be challenged for cause reducing the number available for actual service, there's maybe 40 or 50. Then the striking begins. The defendant -- the state strikes one, the defendant strikes two until only 12 remains. Those 12 constitutes the jury with a trial of a capital case.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right, yes. That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Is there interrogation of the jurors by counsel or by the Court?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: By both.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Oh, prior to this --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- preemptory striking?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- Individually?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes. The defendant can inquire, and the state may inquire and examine the jurors and I understand the Court can excuse anyone that the Court feels does not qualify to serve. Now, the Supreme Court of Alabama found that there are usually Negroes on the venire. Negroes are usually in the jury box and called but the Negroes are always stricken and no Negro has ever served on a petit jury in that county. And the Supreme Court of Alabama held that the fact that the prosecution peremptorily strikes every Negro from the jury panel in the case where the defendant is a Negro does not constitute a violation of the defendant's constitutional rights of due process or equal protection of the law. Clearly is felt --

turns.name: JUSTICE GOLDBERG
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: JUSTICE GOLDBERG
turns.text: Do you attack only the petit jury or do you attack the grand jury also?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: We attack the grand jury also. We say that, that's our second point here and that is that the grand jury and the petit jury drawn from the same box but what you have here is few Negroes in that box because the jury commissioners have not carried out their constitutional duty to fairly acquaint themselves with all qualified jurors in the county. They have a very haphazard method of getting names in that box which favors a larger proportion of Whites and qualified Negroes are obviously not put in the box. They have a small number to start with. Now, as I said, this record clearly established that in every case, Negroes are struck from the venire and the prosecutor or the Solicitor's own testimony is that whenever he has a case he goes over to the lawyer in the other side. He says, “Look, we have some Negroes here. Do you want to use Negroes?” And if the defense counsel says no and he invariably does and if the Solicitor doesn't want to use them as he says, “We strike them first.” In other words we get -- rid of Negroes. Now, the prosecutors never struck any Negro because he felt that a particular Negro had a bias against the defendant or the state. He never struck any Negro because he felt that a particular Negro or several Negroes on that venire would not afford a fair and impartial administration of justice. His only consideration --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: But those reasons would be all be for cause, I suppose if you could show --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, but --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: But we're talking now about preemptory challenges and I -- that the very word preemptory as well as the history of preemptory challenges indicates that I had thought that the person could be as capricious, whimsical, arbitrary or prejudiced as he wanted to be --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- in challenging the jury.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: But there are some instances where the Solicitor cannot prove bias, you say. And if he attempts to prove in a case that a particular Negro might be biased in favor of the defendant because he is Negro and fails, he may then challenge that Negro peremptorily and that's not because of the Negro's race. He's then using the preemptory challenge in the manner in which it was intended. And that is to eliminate bias which you could not prove on which you merely have a suspicion or a whim and you get rid of the Negro that way. But --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: But -- we always did use the preemptory challenges that way (Inaudible). I thought may be he looked at -- maybe sort of a stance and the discussion. If I had a challenge left, of course in my jurisdiction we had six challenges, they didn't have unlimited challenges --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: -- you do in Alabama. But do you mean to say here that the lawyer before you can use a preemptory challenge that shows grounds like bias.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: No. I think that it is not absolute in that sense. That was never the original purpose of the challenge. Its purpose was to secure an impartial jury and to provide for the elimination of bias and it wasn't -- I guess it is used in the manner which you suggest and wholly arbitrarily. But I don't think that that was the original intention of it. I think it's always been limited by the necessity of a fair and impartial jury for the defendant and the public's interest in the impartial administration of justice. So that in every case I would think that we have -- we would have the burden of course of establishing that race was the motive and it maybe difficult in some cases. But the preemptory challenge we say cannot be used to and affect defeat the decisions of this Court construing the Equal Protection Clause or to violate the federal statute which says, that no state officer may exclude Negroes from the jury on account of race and that --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: But suppose (Inaudible)?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well again, the question would be whether there -- that the challenges have been used to discriminate against the whole racial group in the community. If he struck Negroes because they have to use six challenges but whenever he struck, he always struck Negro. We might be able to prove that even in such a case the prosecutor's motive was racial discrimination.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: By definition (Inaudible)? I thought your position in the state was that (Inaudible)?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, our position is that the prosecutor here had a policy of excluding Negroes. He did it through the use of preemptory strike. He says so because I always strike Negroes. I go overnight, talk to the other side and I -- we come to an agreement and we take the Negroes off. And this record shows that his use of the strikes was to affect racial discrimination. This Court has ruled that they cannot exclude Negroes from the grand jury panel or the venire and it shows that they made some attempt here to put some Negroes on. But he used this to affect racial discrimination. And our point is that any state prosecutor who uses his preemptory challenges to affect this result, the exclusion of the whole racial group violates the Equal Protection Clause.

turns.name: JUSTICE GOLDBERG
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Oh yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE GOLDBERG
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, that -- that's our second point as I pointed out before that the striking is facilitated by the fact that the jury originally composed that it's a box as a result of racial discrimination. I think the record is clear here that Negroes were about 26% of the males in that county over 21. And in other words 26% Negroes were potentially qualified but the largest percent that had ever appeared on a grand jury panel or petit jury panel was 10%, 12% or 15%.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Other qualifications in addition (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- 21, weren't they?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right. But we said -- what I'm saying is that there were 26% potentially qualified.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: We put on testimony to show there were other qualified Negro. I think about 13 Negroes out of which 12 were qualified, met all the other qualifications. And the one -- jury commissioner even admitted that there were probably Negroes qualified who are not in there because he had made no effort to ascertain all qualified persons. And the statute certainly requires that. So that it's clear by their own admission that there were Negroes outside of this box who were not qualified. And this small number of qualified Negroes being put in the box facilitated the striking when it came to that. So you can tie these two things together of course, and show that this is clearly a case where you have a state policy of excluding Negroes from their proper participation in the administration of justice and participation in the jury system.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Is there only men jurors in Alabama?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right, they exclude women.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Senator, may I ask you this question please. I don't know that I quite have your point yet. But assuming that this jury was intended legally in all respects and they came to the courtroom and the prosecutor used his challenges, got his preemptory challenges for the purpose of eliminating Negroes from the jury. Do you then say that that is a state action and that --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- that that's discrimination and this case should be reversed?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes. If the prosecutor always strikes Negroes, if race is a criterion and it -- and we show that they always strike Negroes. And no Negro ever serves because of his policy of striking all Negroes that would violate the Equal Protection Clause.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: That's another way of saying that (Inaudible) preemptory challenges that they're exercising (Inaudible).

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: (Inaudible) preemptory challenge, you cannot have it (Inaudible) constitution and the state cannot have a preemptory challenge (Inaudible)?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: The state cannot -- yes, the state cannot use its preemptory challenge to exclude Negroes solely because they're Negroes. Because then the state is using a racial criterion for selecting the members of the jury.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Supposing that you're argument goes farther than that. That a state allows any preemptory challenges at all then you have to assume that some people are going to be prejudiced against Negroes. Some lawyers or their client are not going -- or want Negroes on their jury for a very a prejudicial, capricious and arbitrary reason and that the state therefore is authorizing that could be done whether it be --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- by the prosecutor or by defense counsel or in a civil case.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right, yes. Because this jury selection is an important part of the administration of justice. It's a -- an integral part of the system of justice. And we're saying that race cannot play any part in the administration of justice or the jury selection process.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Just go ahead.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes. If I say even in the case of the defendant. I don't think that race can be allowed as a criteria and even by the defendant because --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: What if the defendant so thinks that, say there's a Negro defendant and thinks that a Negro would be -- just because he's a Negro would be biased against the Negro defendant and it's not just that -- I mean the -- the fact that he's a Negro means bias to him --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: -- and isn't that --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: -- a little bit different?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, that's different because he's eliminating him for bias --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Alright, now --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: -- which he affects --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: -- let's say the prosecutor. Prosecutor eliminates a juror because he's a Negro, only because he's a Negro but only because he thinks that this particular Negro might be biased.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right. He can use his preemptory challenge to eliminate a Negro that he suspects of bias.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Alright, but it's only -- the only reason he suspects him is because he's a Negro?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, I think there would be cases where that would arise out of the facts in the case. He (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Certainly does in the defendant's case. That's --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: No, no. In the defendant's case he had no reason other than race. He wasn't suspecting bias. He wasn't eliminating Negroes as a part of his file, strategy or anything like that.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I know, but that's -- in the case of the defendant exercising his preemptory challenges. When he says, “The reason I'm going to strike this one, this Negro, is just because he's a Negro. I think he's -- because I think he would be bias. That's the only reason I think it would be bias because he's a Negro.”

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, what he suspects as definite bias I would think with respect to himself, he maybe White and he says, “Well, I don't want a Negro on my jury because I am charged with burning down a Negro's house, and this Negro may be biased against me. So I'm going to get him off the jury.” Now, he's suspecting a definite bias. He may not be able to prove it because he puts the Negro on and questions him and he denies it because I can fairly decides this case --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But your point is on the prosecution side, (Inaudible) -- exercising any preemptory challenges is that it may be that you couldn't successfully challenge every preemptory --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: -- challenge that a prosecutor made just because the fellow was a Negro.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: He may not be able to do that but it's certainly true you say that every Negro on every jury --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: -- for years and years couldn't have been -- shouldn't be counted because he's a Negro.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right. From that kind of pattern we can infer that racial discrimination was his motive --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But why (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: -- and there's a case where he admits that as here.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Why does the defend -- what kind of -- how come the prosecutor gets this kind of an answer when he asked the defendant -- defense counsel, “Do you want Negroes on the panel,” and the -- and is this true that the defense counsel answers, “No, I don't want Negro from the panel.”

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's what he said. That's his testimony on page 20 and 27 of the record. He said, they always say “No, I (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But he never got any (Inaudible)?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right. He says -- and he says, “The only variation was once we had a Negro accused of murdering another Negro.” And he wanted to vary this pattern by offering him an all-Negro jury. And in other words, he always took up race as a primary consideration with respect to the composition of every jury.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, now do you have any -- are there any figures or any evidence this record as to how many times or what part of the Negroes who were thrown off or thrown off by the defense over the years?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: No, it doesn't indicate that precisely --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I assume there's someone out there.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: How about in this case?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: In this particular (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Were all the Negroes eliminated from the --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, by the --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: -- from the federal --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: -- by the Solicitor. You see in the instant case there was a venire of a 100 that's a stipulation, eight Negroes, six were available. The prosecutor struck all six.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: So that -- although and so he had to do it three times. He got two off first?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Right. Well, I don't know exactly how they got them off but he was -- with his --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But at least that --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: -- six strikes, he had one at a time --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: -- at least none of them were -- none of them was eliminated by the defense?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right. No -- that's right. And this was the practice in civil cases also. There were lawyers who testified that in every civil case they did this too. So with a general community pattern here --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, was this question asked in this case of defense counsel? Do you want any Negroes on the jury?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: I don't believe (Inaudible). I'd like to --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Senator, do you happen to -- do you have to go as far as you do to prevail in this case? Do you have to go as far as you have just gone in making this particular argument in order to prevail?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, I don't understand. As far as I've gone, I don't --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, what I mean is this, do you have the premises entirely upon the use of preemptory challenges by the prosecution in order to win this case?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Oh no, we can win on the second point which is that the organization of the grand jury and petit jury from the start were the products of racial discrimination.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: So we've heard very little about that.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes. Well, I -- we have it in our brief. I -- we referred to it that what happened here was that the prosec -- for the jury commissioners had a very haphazard method --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes, (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: -- which favored Whites. As a result, very few Negroes actually got in that box. I'd like to save the three minutes I have for rebuttal.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Hall.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, members of the Court. I'm quite surprised that Mrs. Motley didn't go into the other questions. She saves three minutes for that. I'm also surprised that she spent so much time on this particular thing. Because it's been pointed out time and time again that various Courts throughout the country including a Court from Mr. Justice Clark's own state that the matter of preemptory challenges is a manner that it is reserved exclusively for counsel on each side. They don't have to assign any reason. The counsel does not have to say, why he's challenging a person when he is exercising his preemptory challenge. He may not as one of the justices pointed out, may not like the color of the tie of the person's wearing. He may not like the color of his shirt he's wearing. He may not like the color of his eyes. It reminds me of a case that I was retained on back in 1936 when I first started practicing law down in Baldwin County, Alabama. There was a lawyer up in Mobile by the name of Henry Aldrich who was not too well acquainted with the residence in Baldwin County. And that particular case involved and a ejectment who had decided he was going to takeover a piece of land down there in the swamps up above the -- in the Tensaw River, up above Callaway and that goes over to Mobile. Being a native of Baldwin County Henry Aldrich who was one of my classmates asked me if I would associate with him for the purpose of striking the jury and that was the only reason that I was hired in that particular case was to strike the jury. My purpose in that case was to strike all county farmers, renters, share croppers and any other person that had no interest in owning property. When we got through the strike in the jury we round up with the jury of all land owners. The case was trialed for three days. At the end of the trial of the case, we won the case. We did not have to assign a reason why we struck any by the oath of that jury. There's no reason in the world why I have to assign when I am prosecuting a case or defending a case why I'm striking a Negro or I'm striking a Mexican, why I am striking a China man or anybody else of a jury as long as I'm right exercising my preemptory challenge. Now, of course when I get down to the point of striking for cause then I have to have a good reason. And I think all the members of the court know that. I think all the members of the court have trialed cases from time to time. What -- his -- Judge Grooms was pointed out in a class action that was brought down in Birmingham two and a half years ago when it was trialed with reference to Jefferson County in particular and then later on back in October and I was in that case with regard to the (Inaudible). Judge Grooms in the decision just handed down the other day on the second of December said “The number of preemptory challenges in both civil and criminal cases is fixed by statute. The exercise of such preemptory charges is resting exclusively with the parties.”

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: May I ask you to remind me in telling this (Inaudible) you have a special law in Talladega County with reference to capital cases and how many jurors you'll draw or is it carried by general of state laws?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Mr. Justice Black the manner of filling the jury roll and filling the jury box is governed by a special statute in Talladega County but the general law in Alabama still applies as far as the selection of the members of the panel of the venire is concerned.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, you have a capital case, let's say you have a capital case --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes, Your honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- in Talladega. How many jurors would you have to have there, parties to be strike on and -- under your law.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: The judges are required to -- normally he has a venire of say of 50 or 60 and then he is required to draw up to a 100.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Oh, it is a minimum. This is not in the statute, I thought.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: That's right, that's great.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What is the minimum?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: A 100 on a capital case.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You have to summon and have a 100 juror.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What's the object to that statement?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: I don't know what the object is. It's been a traditional from way back, you know when you practice some law down there it was --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I suppose the object was you get both parties strike a lot of people so that they can come as near getting a fair jury responsible.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well of course we have a lot of people that come in who were exempt for various reasons. We have almost say a different categories of people who exempt from jury duty.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well that wasn't the point. How many did you get to strike from in a civil case?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: In a civil case?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Normally about 40, 30 or 40 because in a civil case you strike one for one.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: One for one --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: We had 24 men, that's the rule as I understand.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That's because that's a civil, it's not very important. How many do you have to have in order to strike in connection with a felony that's not a capital case.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well, they usually have maybe 40 to 50 There is no particular set number. Now (Voice Overlap) there was a point, he was asked if --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- to get the idea, you do not have a statute to that which relates to the capital cases only which was passed for the expressed service trying to see that there's enough men there so that when you get through striking they could come as near as possible to getting a fair jury.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well now, it has been my experience with that particular thing though sometimes we ran out of a number of people, a lot of them have asked to be excused for various reasons that some of them don't believe in capital punishment. Some of them are --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I understand that but is there not a minimum in capital cases that you have to have, is that the statute --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Not that I know of, no.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You don't know.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, there is a larger number though that we have to have.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well, what -- what normally happens in the case and it has been my experience and I've tried them in various places in Alabama is when you run down to say about 40 then the judge goes out and has the sheriff, summons people from around the courthouse and from around the business.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You do need to start with more juries in the capital case and other cases?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes sir, yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I thought in your statement you started with a minimum of 100?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well that's what they normally summons, yes sir. It starts with a 100. But a lot of them come in and say we don't want to serve, we are over age, we are railroad engineers, we are conductor on trains, we are telegraph operator. We are teachers, we are doctors and --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And that excuses although it's --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And show that they come (Inaudible) the statute. Is it that?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: And some of them are hard in hearing and so forth.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Then if it gets down below what the statutes says it has to have to get them into a strike, it is send down and get what you call (Inaudible).

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: (Inaudible), yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Go down in summons them and bring them in.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Sheriff --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That's the system you have here is they get -- the defendant gets two strikes and the other -- on this mark.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: In -- in criminal cases.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: In the civil cases that's one on one.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: In some of those legislation that was peculiar to Talladega County unlike with the petitioner's brief in the appendix they have the main statutory provisions is that on the top of the 8 (a) that the -- that the special legislation begins which is peculiar to this county, well, and goes all the way through the -- through 11 (a).

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: So far in the appendix?

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Appendix to the petitioner's brief.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: There are set out that various constitutional provisions in Alabama statutes and that most of them seems to be of general application and I'm asking whether the ones of exclusive application to this county begin at the top of 8 (a) and end at 11 (a) that's in the information.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: That is correct sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Is that it?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Mr. Hall can you explain to us why was this thing point -- pointed out that the Commission (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well now I think the commissioners did the best they could under the circumstances to --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Now, I'm going to the board now (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: I didn't understand the Alabama Supreme Court to say that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well you might be right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well now I want to be practical about this thing. Yes, and I think it's impossible for any jury commission to go out and get every male citizen between 21 and 65 whether he is White or whether he is Negro. It is impossible to do it. They go out and do the best they can with the facilities that are available to them.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well that was to say that the Commissioner in saying that he didn't know any Negroes and therefore he did not put him on the panel.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: I think you found there by reading this record here that they made a determinant effort by doing --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: No, ask -- I didn't say that. I asked you that question. I asked you if that would justify the Commissioner in saying “Well, I did know any Negroes or I didn't know whether there are any qualified or not so I didn't put any Negroes on the panel.”

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well, your honor that is a hypothetical question --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Beg your pardon --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: I'll say and I think there is a hypothetical question.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: It is a hypothetical question.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Could they do that?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: I'm not going to say, whether they could or couldn't do it but I'm talking about this particular case Mr. Chief Justice

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Or wasn't there -- didn't one of the Commissioner say what was almost tantamount to what I've said.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well, he might have said it but the other said that they went out into their plants. They looked over the board of registration list. They conducted planned marriages which have both Negroes and Whites. They've contacted the -- various farm organizations that Negroes and Whites belong to --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well did they? Wasn't there one who at least who said that he did not go to any of the --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well he's going to bring them into the (Voice Overlap) anything with just one man.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- and didn't go to any of the Negro clubs or organizations but he did go to the Whites.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: It didn't say he went to the White organizations.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I understood that he did.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well you just see they didn't know of any Negro organizations now, let's put it that way.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Or churches?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: That's' right.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: They didn't know of any Negro churches.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well he might have known some Negro churches, that may have been one man but there are three members of the jury commission in this.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I know but one apple can spoil a box you know too.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well, that's true.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes, I understand you. Now, that -- that same thing would apply to White people.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Now the proof of the evidence under the (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well I think you found that the Talladega County has in much better shape than some of the other counties in Alabama and of course that particular situation is concerned.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well I'm not going to make any admissions but I know that there are some counties here that we have been trying to work on to try to get them to improve the situation down there. And so that cases like this won't come up here and I've been one of those that has been really working on that particular situation and I feel like Talladega County is in much better shape and mostly all of the rest of the counties except Jefferson County. Jefferson County is in good shape. Butler County is in better shape than it was. Mobile County is in good shape. Baldwin County is in good shape.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: So you're saying that (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: There were two Negroes that served on the grand jury that's been indicted this man.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well now, I can't be responsible for that. Now if you will -- if you will -- it even depends on prosecuting the case. You would use your own judgment as to whom you were going to strike off, wouldn't you? And you wouldn't ask anybody's opinion about it, would you?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: We've had some served down in -- in Butler County and Greenville, Alabama. Recently --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, let's take this county as far as you know and as far as any of the witnesses in this case knew has there ever been a Negro set on a jury in this county in either a civil or criminal case?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: On a petit jury?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Petit jury, yes sir.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Not that I know of, the record they didn't show it.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: The record shows to the contrary that that is not correct.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes -- that's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: It's been (Inaudible). On the jury panel that served --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: That they're only venire, yes sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Quite necessary, that over the years than the Negro on a petit jury.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: I've never tried a case in that county, I don't know.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: You have no idea that --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Right. I have a bit of good idea from my own experience and find cases in Virginia in the District of Columbia and now in Alabama and representing that because I was -- say that I have a very good idea of what.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Well a lot of county in the region?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well, I've explained that in my brief. Every time I represent one of the defendant and he said that I don't want a Negro sitting on the jury and trying me.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Well I know but in this case and there is much better cases that's the Solicitor General who likes the Negroes. Why do you -- supposed the Negro -- this just strikes the Negroes in every single case. There is no --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: I haven't discussed that with the Solicitor. I don't' have any idea.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: That's the use of this brand new challenge.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: He then -- he don't have to assign a reason, does he Mr. Justice Clark?

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: What about the grand jury?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well of course the grand jury is concerned, the judge who's names out of the hat. Do that mean look at them? There is no distinction as far as the colors of the cards, the textures of the cards, there is no marked on the cards, or anything, you start forming them out until it gets 18 men.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Where did you get the cards?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: It gets the cards out of the jury box. The jury box is locked up until it's brought into the courtroom.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: We have it instead of a wheel.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: No, we don't have wheel. We've got a -- a box that it's kind of like a safety deposit box, a steel box that has kept locked and kept in the probate judges office.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: It's hard getting the box.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: They come off the jury room.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: The -- in this particular county and some -- most all or every county in Alabama, the second clerk is automatically the clerk of the jury commissioner and he operates, he or she operates under the directions of the jury commissioner. In Jefferson County they have a full time clerk and --

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: And what about this county?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: In this county the second clerk access the clerk of this jury.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: They have three Commissioners.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: They have three Commissioners appointed by the government.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: There's not the -- the one to go in the box for storage?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: They go out and try to find the names of qualified men to go into the -- on the jury roll first and then after we go in the jury roll the jury box is made up, from the names on the jury roll--

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Are you familiar with Cassell, a case down in Texas?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Well, this seems like a legislature procedure.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well no. I can explain it to you a little bit more in detail, they've got a great, big book and they put down the names by precincts in the various areas of the county. And then they take those names off and put them on cards and put those cards in a box and the box is locked up. Once those names are drawn out they're not used again for two more years and those names are separated from the other names that are in the jury box.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: How many names are put in the box?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well it depends on how many names are on the roll. They put every one of the names that are on the roll.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: And they run down the rolls --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Line the lines --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes sir. That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: As what you said they went around the county and like you said --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: They do in making up the rule, but they make up the rule every two years.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: They make up the roll a bit.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes. They make up the roll and then their cards come off the roll.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Did they have a message caller?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Sir?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: They call it that in (Inaudible) amount of time.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Not now. There was a time back there in Scottsboro cases when it was and even some of the candidates happened to recently and told the Attorney General, Alabama Attorney General's office got after about that thing when they -- they stopped doing it but they don't do it anymore as far as I know.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: The -- excuse me?

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: What do you say about the percentage?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Are you talking about the Talladega County?

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: We wrap up specific claims in the (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well, now --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Calling people's name in the whole jury box.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: The Solicitor attempted to go into that and explain why. They want more Negroes names on the roll and consequently in the box and even into some things that I don't particularly condone as far as incidence of sexual diseases and illegitimacy and things of that sort I've come to say, but that is in the record and I have to admit it it's in there.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What about the -- what about the educational --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well the educational qualifications -- they work a whole lot new with, Mr. Justice Black.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: In that county how about -- what what's the ratio of it there.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well, I don't know if this record shows it now but you will find it almost in every county in Alabama the ratio of dropouts and those are starting into 1st grade, who finish in the 12th grade as compared with the Whites about 90% of the Whites finished and about 50% of the Negroes finished.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Are -- are there many of the local Negroes that go through those to the Talladega County and take advantage of that.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Not (Inaudible), they come from other parts of the state.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You have good schools in Talladega.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Very good schools, yes sir. Very good schools in Tuskegee

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And it's a compulsory education there.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It is important --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: But a lot of those get the education and we find it is true. Almost -- in all of these rural areas of Alabama, those that do get the education leave. They don't stay. They go to North. They go to Detroit, in New York and Chicago and various other places. So that it leaves the old people. The ones that either illiterate or infirmed or too old or something of that sort, so we don't have to (Inaudible) other than school teachers and school teachers invariably claim their exemption and of course most of the school teachers are women and then outlive what the jury says.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What did your commissioner say about knowing the call of people in this county?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well they all know practically all of them in -- in the rural counties.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did they testify -- to making it testify --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Two of them -- I think maybe all three of them, do you remember the issue about it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: All testified.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Do you have a statutory test and I'm reading now from the bottom of 4 (a) and the top of 5 (a) of the appendix to the petitioner's brief but requiring to assess in order to be eligible for jury duty of men that -- be over 21 and each -- as to generate it, it reputed to be honest and intelligent and esteemed in the community for its integrity, good character and sound judgment. I supposed it's not only a matter of perhaps White jury commissioners not being acquainted with negros but perhaps also a manner that because of 75 years of more thought patterns and social customs down there they -- they are perhaps would be fewer negros who had that general reputation in the community at large but for those qualities and that would be quite acquainted, is that right.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: I know that because -- if I am guilty of various disqualifying pranks, of course that's true of a lot of White people too but we would find the percentages larger among the Negro population and then you do among the white --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- a general refute for qualities of honest, intelligent, esteemed and --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: No, I'm not trying to cast any displacement from this, as a matter of fact --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- matter of a fact whether or not the jury commissioners would -- whether there would be as many proportionately as many Negroes who had this estimation of the community at large, as there would be --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: I don't think so.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You'd say with the two on this grand jury, is that true of (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: There were two among this grand jury that indicted this man for rape over a White girl.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- and summon all the grand juries have been you know statistically so you know the statistics on them whether or not they summoned each grand jury.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well they have been summoned quite a few grand juries, there has been some grand juries that haven't had Negroes on them. But what in a particular reason why there happened to be on this -- this grand jury, they -- they just -- that's the way the ball bounced.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: To mention, they don't put a cetain percentage of --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: They don't put anybody on the grand jury. The judge pulls the names of the --

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: No, I mean on the -- on the roll, they don't put a certain percentage on the roll of Negro, is that right on the --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: They don't make any effort to do that, no sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: This just comes out with certain percentage.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Mr. Hall, is the -- does the same, is the same striking procedure used statewide? You start out with a number of jurors and then there is the alternate strike when you get to the jury.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: That's statewide?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: And does -- does striking has to stop when you get to 12.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: That's right and of course if you --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: You can't go on striking and enforce the calling of more jurors.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Oh, when you get down to 12 that's your jury?

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: That's the end of it?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: No more strike that's what you say.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: That's right. (Voice Overlap) Of course, you've got this -- you got the challenges per call at first.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Oh, I understand.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That's all that have been left in the box.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But it happened to, you don't have enough jurors over --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Then you can go out and get some (Inaudible), that's right. And from the area around the court that was where they usually do it, you know?

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Now, the question that the -- that the -- the evidence in this case is that the prosecutor usually ask to the defense counsel if he wants Negroes on the jury that was a testimony during the case.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: There is some testimony to that effect, yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And the -- but that question was not asked in this case.

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: And whether that was asked in this particular case, the record doesn't show it as far as I know.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: But you agree that all of the strikes of this case were made by the -- of Negroes were made by the prosecution?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: It is possible, where they – because --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Can't you -- can you tell from the record or you can't?

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Well if I were -- the prosecutor probably do the same thing.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Oh, I know but I -- I just wondered as a matter of fact on this record, does it show that all of the Negroes who were stricken were stricken by the prosecution, is that --

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: I don't know whether the record shows that or not.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LESLIE HALL
turns.text: Is there --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Senator Motley.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: I think Mr. Justice Black if you would looked at Section 63 of the Title, I don't have it here. I'm referring to the Alabama Code Title 30 Section 63. I thought perhaps we had cited in our briefs somewhere but that's the statute I believe you have in mind which sets a minimum number of juries to be called in a capital case of 50 and no more than a 100. It says the circuit clerk show directly share the summon at least 50 and no more than a 100 in a capital case. I think if you looked at that that was the section. Now --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- the felony was then kind of a --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: No, not just capital cases. There is a separate section on felonies in general. Now in this case, I think because we mentioned previously it's clear that the jury commissioners whose job was to secure names for the jury roll which was then put in the jury box relied on their acquaintances, business acquaintances and social acquaintances primarily in securing these names. One jury commissioners had been pointed out and even know the percentage of Negroes in the county he estimated it at about 10% where actually it's 32%, 26% being the number of Negro males over 21, so that he had no idea of the number of Negroes in the community and then one jury commissioner on page 95 of the record he was asked, “Have you gone to any purely Negro organizations for a list of names?” And he said, “No.” The jury clerk who assisted the process of getting the names said she knew a couple of Negroes and this was the extent of anyone's direct testimony as to knowing any particular Negroes. She said, “I asked Bob Strickland and principle may vary, they send me a list and I asked another man and he never sent it.” And whenever a jury commissioner was asked to name some Negroes he put on the jury list. He couldn't even do it as familiarity with Negroes was so limited.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: It's an ideal world, he wouldn't be able to do it, would he? He should know both --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, he should have some system whereby all qualified persons I would think are placed on the jury roll as the statute here required.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: But he should be (Inaudible).

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well yes. He shouldn't have --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: He should know, I put so many Negroes on.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well he should be able to name some. There are large numbers of Negroes as he claimed that had been put on here, he ought to be able to call a few not that he could name every single one.

turns.name: JUSTICE GOLDBERG
turns.text: But what -- what you mean is he should follow the law in --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right. What he should do is --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- there is trial below and that run in the trial with (Inaudible) went direct to the Negroes and put them on the list of liberty.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, that's right. I'm not saying he should go out and say, “Well, I've got six Negroes I'm going to put them in the box.”

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But he started a pretty tough job there.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Not and (Inaudible). Well he had -- they elect and many left where it is likely to grow at the same time. You must have somebody on there show the county how it is going either, so he's really got to precept (Inaudible) but --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well I think the best way that -- to get around that is to have an impartial method whereby you select a large number of qualified persons. They have no systematic method for getting the names and that would set a couple --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: But it -- you suggest that it would be appropriate to -- to go to all Negro organizations to -- to (Inaudible).

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well if that's the way or method of getting it, if that's the method, it's to get names from organizations only then I say they should go to Negro and White organizations if this is your method.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: But they didn't have that method particularly, they sometimes went to the jury to the telephone booth, sometimes in the city directory and so forth. They failed to have any system for getting these names and that led them into trouble and it might --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Voice overlap) just took some out of each page. Is that in demand --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: No, whatever method they used if they used it as the method and that method was used on Negroes and Whites alike, of course that would be all right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But you explain then that the Negroes didn't have a cell phone or --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well if that were a fact that they knew, that happened to be the fact in that community. That there was, if they knew this and this was a matter of which they used to get what it sounds like. I believe my time is over.
